---
title: "Questions: Fatal vs. Survivable Triage"
date: "2026-08-10"
---

# Questions: Fatal vs. Survivable Triage {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Week Eight, Lab Eight, part nine of ten. In part eight we built the timed rehearsal and the cut line. Now we prepare for the question period — the part most students fear and the part you can actually control. The questions you will get are not a surprise; they are the rows of the threats-and-responses table you wrote weeks ago. This segment turns that table into prepared answers and gives you the single distinction that keeps you composed when a question lands hard.
:::

## The Questions Are Your Threats Table

- The hard questions are the **rows of your Ch 7.5 threats table**
- Each attacks your identifying assumption at a named weak point
- Rehearse a four-part answer: threat, signature, what you did, residual
- "Yes, that's the central worry — if it were driving this we'd see X…"
- Conceding what remains makes you look **more** credible, not less

::: notes
The questions you will get are the rows of your threats-and-responses table from Chapter Seven-point-five. Every hard question has the same shape: isn't your estimate just picking up some confounder rather than the effect you claim? That is your identifying assumption being attacked at a weak point you already named. So before the talk, turn each row into a prepared four-part answer, rehearsed in this order: the threat — concede it is the central worry, do not dismiss it; the signature — if the confounder were driving the result, what would we see; what you did — the test or design choice from column three; and the residual concern — what column four honestly admits remains. Said live: yes, that's the central worry; if it were driving this, we'd see this signature; we checked, and we see this reassuring result; what I still can't fully rule out is this residual, though here is a bound on how bad it could be. That answer makes you look more credible than a confident dismissal, because it shows you saw the problem before they did.
:::

## Fatal vs. Survivable

- A **survivable** critique attacks a number: "cluster differently," "winsorize at 1%"
- Answer: "we checked; it doesn't" — point at robustness, or re-run and report
- A **fatal** critique attacks the comparison: "you were already diverging"
- If a fatal critique is right, **do not defend the indefensible** — concede and retreat
- "I don't know" *with structure* proves your other answers are trustworthy

::: notes
This is the distinction that lets you keep composure: a survivable critique attacks a number; a fatal critique attacks the comparison. A survivable critique asks whether a choice changes the answer — cluster differently, winsorize at one percent not five, control for firm size. You answer "we checked; it doesn't," point at the robustness panel, or say "fair — let me re-run it and report back." The estimate might move a little; it does not vanish. A fatal critique attacks the identifying assumption itself — your treated and control groups were already diverging before treatment, your instrument plausibly affects the outcome directly. If the attacker is right, no amount of re-clustering saves you. The hardest discipline in the camp: do not defend the indefensible. Concede at the level the critique was made and retreat to what survives — this is a strong association consistent with the mechanism, not the clean causal estimate I'd hoped for, and that's a real limitation. A conceded fatal critique is a wounded paper but an intact scientist, and the retreat almost always lands somewhere real — a robust pattern future work should test causally is a genuine contribution. Know your retreat position before you walk in. And for the question you genuinely cannot answer, say "I don't know" with structure — that's outside what my design can speak to; my guess is this direction, but I'd want this specific check first. A located "I don't know" proves the rest of your "I know"s are trustworthy.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The hard questions are your threats-table rows — answer each in four parts. Triage live: **survivable attacks a number** (point at robustness), **fatal attacks the comparison** (concede and retreat to the descriptive claim). Next: the checklist and the capstone.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part nine: the hard questions are the rows of your threats table, and each gets a four-part answer that concedes the worry, names the signature, states what you did, and admits the residual. Triage in real time: a survivable critique attacks a number, and you point at robustness; a fatal critique attacks the comparison, and if it is right you concede and retreat to the descriptive claim that survives, never bluffing. And a structured "I don't know" is a strength, not a failure. In part ten we close the lab and the camp — the submission checklist a reviewer runs, the connection to Assessment Eight, and the single sentence this whole eight weeks existed to let you say.
:::
